findings.evidence: The PR claims to make validate_email accept apostrophes (Ticket #88). The _EMAIL_RE pattern in validation.py is identical in base and head: r"^[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$" — the apostrophe character (') is not added to the character class. validate_email("o'brien@example.com") still returns False after this PR.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: The new test (tests/test_utils.py:52-56) uses the tautological assertion `assert result in (True, False)`. A bool is always in (True, False), so the test passes whether the validator accepts or rejects the apostrophe address. The test is incapable of falsifying the fix.
findings.fix: Add the apostrophe to _EMAIL_RE: change r"[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+" to r"[A-Za-z0-9._%+'\-]+" (or equivalent). Then change the test assertion to `assert result is True` so it actually verifies the fix.

findings.evidence: `assert result in (True, False)` is a tautology: any Python bool satisfies it. The test_validate_email_apostrophe_local_part function (added in this PR) does not assert that validate_email("o'brien@example.com") returns True — only that it returns some boolean. It would pass even if the validator were replaced with `return False` unconditionally.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: The assertion is vacuous by construction — it asserts nothing about the specific expected value, so CI cannot detect the missing fix or any future regression against apostrophe acceptance.
findings.fix: Replace `assert result in (True, False)` with `assert result is True` to make the test a real regression guard for Ticket #88.
